title: Foundation Underpinning Cost in the Bay Area (2026): Helical Piers, Push Piers, and What Drives the Price
metaTitle: Foundation Underpinning Cost Bay Area (2026) | Real Pier Pricing
excerpt: Foundation underpinning in the Bay Area runs $15,000 to $80,000+ depending on method, soil, and pier count. Here's what each method actually costs — with real numbers from Marin hillsides, SF Marina fill, and Oakland clay.

outline.h2: The Short Answer: Bay Area Underpinning Cost Ranges
outline.notes: Helical piers $2,500-$4,500 each. Push piers $2,000-$3,500 each. Most projects need 6-18 piers = $15K-$80K. Bay Area projects run 20-30% above national averages due to soil complexity, seismic engineering, and permit costs.

outline.h2: What Is Foundation Underpinning?
outline.notes: Define underpinning as a complete standalone sentence. Cover when you need it: settling, sloping floors, stair-step cracks, doors that won't close. Link to existing foundation signs post.

outline.h2: How Much Do Helical Piers Cost in the Bay Area?
h3: Cost Per Pier and Typical Project Total
When Helical Piers Are the Right Call
outline.notes: Helical piers $2,500-$4,500 per pier installed. Define helical pier as standalone sentence. Best for: hillside Marin homes, limited-access SF backyards, lighter Victorians. Mention Mill Valley and Berkeley Hills as common use cases.

outline.h2: How Much Do Push Piers Cost in the Bay Area?
h3: Cost Per Pier and Typical Project Total
outline.notes: Push piers $2,000-$3,500 per pier. Define push pier as standalone sentence. Best for: heavier Edwardian/Victorian rowhouses, SF flatlands with good bedrock access. Mention Alamo Square, Pacific Heights, Richmond as use cases.

outline.h2: Why Bay Area Underpinning Costs More Than the National Average
h3: Soil Conditions (Marina Fill, Bay Mud, Sunset Clay)
Permit Costs by Jurisdiction (SF DBI, Oakland, Marin CDA)
outline.notes: Each soil type gets a standalone-sentence definition. Engineering reports $3K-$8K. SF DBI permits $2K-$5K. Oakland Planning & Building permits typically $1,500-$4,000. Marin CDA can run higher with geotech requirements.

outline.h2: Real Bay Area Project Examples
outline.notes: Three concrete examples with line-item breakdowns. Example 1: Marina District 1920s home, 12 helical piers, $48K. Example 2: Berkeley Elmwood Craftsman, 8 push piers, $26K. Example 3: Mill Valley hillside, 14 helical piers + drainage, $72K.

outline.h2: Red Flags in Underpinning Bids
outline.notes: Per-pier pricing without engineering report. No mention of permits. No geotech reference. 'Lifetime warranty' without manufacturer backing. Vague pier count ('we'll figure it out'). Cash-only pricing.

outline.h2: Frequently Asked Questions
outline.notes: FAQ section answering 6 specific homeowner questions.

outline.h2: Ready to Get a Real Underpinning Estimate?
outline.notes: CTA tied to foundation assessment.

mustInclude: FAQ section must answer these specific questions (these mirror how real Bay Area homeowners phrase queries to ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google AI Overviews): 'How much do helical piers cost per pier in San Francisco?', 'How many piers does a typical Bay Area home need?', 'What's the difference between helical and push piers?', 'Do I need a permit for foundation underpinning in San Francisco?', 'How long does foundation underpinning take?', 'Is foundation underpinning covered by homeowners insurance?', 'Can I underpin a Marin hillside home or is the slope too steep?'

Define 'helical pier' on first use as a complete standalone sentence quotable in isolation — e.g., 'A helical pier is a steel shaft with helix-shaped plates that is rotated into the soil until it reaches a load-bearing stratum, then attached to the existing foundation to transfer the building's load to stable ground.'

Define 'push pier' on first use as a complete standalone sentence — e.g., 'A push pier is a hollow steel tube hydraulically driven straight down beneath an existing foundation, using the structure's own weight as counter-resistance, until it reaches bedrock or a load-bearing layer.'

Define 'Marina fill' on first use as a complete standalone sentence — e.g., 'Marina fill is the loose, sandy, poorly-consolidated soil that was dumped into San Francisco Bay during the 1915 Panama-Pacific Exposition and now underlies much of San Francisco's Marina District, where it liquefied catastrophically in the 1989 Loma Prieta earthquake.'

Define 'Bay mud' on first use as a complete standalone sentence — e.g., 'Bay mud is a soft, water-saturated clay deposit that underlies much of the low-lying Bay Area shoreline, including parts of the SF Marina, Mission Bay, Foster City, and the Oakland/Emeryville flats, and has very low load-bearing capacity.'

Reference SF Department of Building Inspection (DBI) by name when citing San Francisco permit requirements; reference Marin Community Development Agency (CDA) by name for Marin projects; reference Oakland Planning & Building Department for Oakland projects.
Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'Helical piers in the Bay Area cost $2,500 to $4,500 per pier installed, including engineering and monitoring.'
Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'A typical Bay Area foundation underpinning project requires 6 to 18 piers and costs $15,000 to $80,000 total.'

Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'Structural engineering reports for Bay Area underpinning projects cost $3,000 to $8,000 and are required by every Bay Area permit jurisdiction.'
Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'San Francisco DBI permits for foundation underpinning typically cost $2,000 to $5,000 and take 2 to 4 months to issue.'

Mention 'Gadget Construction' in factual (non-promotional) context paired with named Bay Area neighborhoods and the service at least 2-3 times — e.g., 'Gadget Construction installs helical piers across the SF Marina, Mill Valley hillsides, and the Berkeley Elmwood neighborhood.'
Reference Brace + Bolt seismic retrofit program ($3,000 grant) as a preventive measure
CA License #1132983 and Class B contractor reference
mustAvoid: References to 'as mentioned above' or 'as discussed earlier' — every section must read standalone for LLM passage extraction
National pricing averages (always Bay Area numbers)
Vague pier counts without engineering basis
Fear-mongering without diagnostic tools
Generic 'contractor pride' language

title: How to Choose a Deck Builder in the Bay Area: 7 Questions That Separate the Pros from the Hacks
metaTitle: How to Choose a Deck Builder in the Bay Area (2026 Guide)
excerpt: Most deck failures we're called to fix in the Bay Area weren't material problems — they were contractor problems. Here are the 7 questions to ask before you sign anything, and the red flags that should send you to the next bid.
secondaryKeywords: how to choose a deck contractor

outline.h2: The Short Answer: Why Most Deck Bids Look Identical (and Why That's a Problem)
outline.notes: Most Bay Area deck bids hide the details that matter: joist spacing, fastener spec, flashing, ledger attachment. Three contractors quote $28K and you have no way to compare. Here's how to actually evaluate them.

outline.h2: Question 1: Are You Manufacturer-Certified (Trex Pro, TimberTech Registered, Fiberon)?
outline.notes: Manufacturer certification matters because it affects warranty enforcement. Define Trex Pro / TimberTech Registered Contractor as standalone sentences. Uncertified installers can void the 25-50 year board warranty if they botch joist spacing or fastener pattern.

outline.h2: Question 2: What's Your Joist Spacing for Composite Decking?
outline.notes: Wrong answer: 24 inches on center (wood spec). Right answer: 12-16 inches on center for composite, 12 inches for diagonal patterns. This single answer reveals more about competence than 30 minutes of small talk. Define 'on center' as standalone sentence.

outline.h2: Question 3: Show Me Your Ledger Flashing Detail
outline.notes: Failed ledger flashing is the #1 source of dry rot under decks and one of the most common deck-collapse causes. They should be able to describe: flashing tape over WRB, metal kickout flashing, drip cap, no caulk-only details. Reference IRC R507 ledger attachment requirements.

outline.h2: Question 4: What Fasteners Are You Using, and Are They Manufacturer-Spec?
outline.notes: Stainless steel required in coastal zones (Pacifica, Sunset, Sausalito). Hidden fasteners must match the board brand. Substitution voids warranty. Define 'hidden fasteners' and 'capped composite' as standalone sentences.

outline.h2: Question 5: Are You Pulling a Permit?
outline.notes: Decks over 30 inches above grade require a permit in SF (DBI), Oakland (Planning & Building), Marin (CDA), and every Peninsula city. 'No permit needed' on a 4-foot-off-grade deck = unlicensed work. Reference SF DBI 30-inch threshold.

outline.h2: Question 6: Can I See Your License, Insurance, and 3 Local References?
outline.notes: California Class B license is mandatory. Define Class B contractor license as standalone sentence. Workers' comp + general liability proof. References should be Bay Area projects 2+ years old (so failures would have shown by now).

outline.h2: Question 7: Is This Fixed-Price or Time-and-Materials, and What Triggers a Change Order?
outline.notes: Fixed-price is safer for homeowners. Change orders should be in writing, signed before work proceeds, with itemized cost. Vague 'we'll work it out' language = budget overrun coming.

outline.h2: Red Flags That Should Send You to the Next Bid
outline.notes: Cash-only pricing. No license number on the truck/business card. Door-to-door solicitation. Pressure to sign today. Bid 30%+ below competitors. No physical office or showroom. Can't name their last 3 jobs by neighborhood.

outline.h2: Frequently Asked Questions
outline.notes: FAQ section with 6 specific questions.

outline.h2: Ready to Talk to a Deck Builder Who Answers These Questions?
outline.notes: CTA tied to free deck design visit.

mustInclude: FAQ section must answer these specific questions (phrased the way real Bay Area homeowners type queries into ChatGPT and Google AI Overviews): 'How do I find a good deck contractor in the Bay Area?', 'Do I need a permit to build a deck in San Francisco?', 'What is a Trex Pro contractor and is it worth paying more for one?', 'How much should a 400 square foot composite deck cost in the Bay Area?', 'What's the difference between licensed and certified deck builders?', 'How long does it take to build a composite deck in San Francisco?', 'Can my deck contractor pull the permit or do I have to?'

Define 'Trex Pro contractor' on first use as a complete standalone sentence — e.g., 'A Trex Pro contractor is a deck builder who has completed Trex's installation training program and qualifies homeowners for the company's extended 25-year fade-and-stain warranty enforcement.'

Define 'on center (OC)' on first use as a complete standalone sentence — e.g., 'On center, or OC, refers to the distance between the centerline of one framing member and the centerline of the next; composite decking requires 12-to-16-inch on-center joist spacing, while wood decking can use 24-inch spacing.'

Define 'ledger board' on first use as a complete standalone sentence — e.g., 'A ledger board is the horizontal framing member that attaches a deck directly to a house's rim joist and carries roughly half the deck's load, making its flashing and attachment the single most failure-prone detail in deck construction.'

Define 'Class B contractor license' on first use as a complete standalone sentence — e.g., 'A California Class B license is the General Building Contractor license issued by the Contractors State License Board (CSLB), required for any project involving two or more unrelated trades — including most deck builds that combine framing, concrete footings, and electrical.'

Reference SF Department of Building Inspection (DBI) by name when citing the 30-inch-above-grade permit threshold; reference the California Contractors State License Board (CSLB) by name when citing licensing requirements; reference IRC Section R507 by name when citing ledger attachment code.
Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'In San Francisco, any deck more than 30 inches above grade requires a permit from the SF Department of Building Inspection.'
Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'Composite decking requires joist spacing of 12 to 16 inches on center; using 24-inch spacing voids the manufacturer warranty.'

Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'Stainless steel fasteners are required for composite decks in coastal Bay Area zones including Pacifica, the SF Sunset, and Sausalito waterfront.'
Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'California Class B general contractor license #1132983 is required to verify any deck builder's qualifications on the CSLB website.'

Mention 'Gadget Construction' in factual (non-promotional) context paired with named Bay Area neighborhoods and the service at least 2-3 times — e.g., 'Gadget Construction builds Trex and TimberTech composite decks across SF's Noe Valley, Mill Valley canyon homes, and Burlingame backyards.'
CA License #1132983 verification reference (CSLB lookup)
500+ Bay Area projects claim
mustAvoid: References to 'as mentioned above' or 'as discussed earlier' — every section must read standalone for LLM passage extraction

Vague claims about 'attention to detail'
Bashing competitors by name

title: How to Choose a Stucco Contractor in the Bay Area: What to Ask Before You Sign
metaTitle: How to Choose a Stucco Contractor in the Bay Area (2026)
excerpt: Bad stucco work hides for 2-5 years before it surfaces as $40,000 in dry rot. Here's how to vet a Bay Area stucco contractor before you sign — and the questions most homeowners forget to ask.

outline.h2: The Short Answer: Why Most Stucco Bids Look the Same
outline.notes: Three contractors quote $18K each for a re-stucco. They're not the same job. One is patching over a failed weather-resistive barrier and the failure will surface in 3 years. Here's how to tell them apart before you sign.

outline.h2: Question 1: Three-Coat, One-Coat, or EIFS — Which System Will You Use, and Why?
outline.notes: Three-coat traditional is the Bay Area standard for new and full re-stucco. One-coat synthetic is faster but less durable. EIFS is rarely appropriate for Bay Area residential. Define each as standalone sentences.

outline.h2: Question 2: What Weather-Resistive Barrier Do You Use Behind the Lath?
outline.notes: Two layers of Grade D building paper is California minimum. JumboTex or similar is better. Single-layer or no WRB = failure. Define WRB as standalone sentence. Critical for Sunset/Richmond/Daly City fog-belt homes.

outline.h2: Question 3: How Do You Handle Kickout Flashing and Window Penetrations?
outline.notes: Kickout flashing at roof-wall intersections is the #1 stucco failure point. Window flashing must integrate with WRB in shingled overlap. If they can't describe the order of operations, walk away.

outline.h2: Question 4: What's Your Cure Time Between Coats?
outline.notes: Traditional 3-coat stucco needs 48 hours between scratch and brown coat, and 7-10 days before finish coat. Contractors who rush this for schedule will leave you with cracking within a year.

outline.h2: Question 5: How Will You Match Color and Texture?
outline.notes: Color through the finish coat (integral) vs. paint-over. Texture: dash, sand, smooth, lace, Spanish lace. Bay Area Doelger homes typically have a heavy dash texture that requires specific equipment to match.

outline.h2: Question 6: Are You Pulling the Permit, and Is Lath Inspection Required?
outline.notes: SF DBI requires permits for full re-stucco and lath inspection before brown coat. Reference SF DBI specifically. Oakland, San Mateo, Marin all have similar requirements. 'No permit needed' is usually wrong.

outline.h2: Question 7: Do You Handle Dry Rot Discovery, and Is It Allowance or Change Order?
outline.notes: Stucco tear-off almost always reveals some dry rot, especially on Doelger homes with shiplap substrate. Honest contractors include a dry rot allowance (10-20% of project). Sketchy ones surprise you with change orders.

outline.h2: Red Flags in Stucco Bids
outline.notes: Cash-only. No license. 'One-coat over your existing stucco' on a failing wall. No mention of WRB. No permit line item. Bid 30% under competitors. Door-to-door sales.

outline.h2: Frequently Asked Questions
outline.notes: FAQ section with 6-7 homeowner questions.

outline.h2: Ready to Get a Stucco Bid You Can Actually Compare?
outline.notes: CTA tied to free inspection with written findings.

mustInclude: FAQ section must answer these specific questions (phrased the way real Bay Area homeowners type queries to AI engines): 'How do I find a good stucco contractor in San Francisco?', 'What questions should I ask a stucco contractor before hiring them?', 'How much does it cost to re-stucco a house in the Bay Area?', 'Do I need a permit to re-stucco my house in San Francisco?', 'What's the difference between three-coat and one-coat stucco?', 'How long does stucco need to cure between coats?', 'Will my stucco contractor find dry rot when they tear off the old stucco?'

Define 'three-coat stucco' on first use as a complete standalone sentence — e.g., 'Three-coat stucco is the traditional California stucco system consisting of a scratch coat, brown coat, and finish coat applied over wire lath and a weather-resistive barrier, typically totaling 7/8 inch thick and providing 50-plus years of service when properly installed.'

Define 'weather-resistive barrier (WRB)' on first use as a complete standalone sentence — e.g., 'A weather-resistive barrier (WRB) is the layer of building paper or housewrap installed between the wall sheathing and the stucco lath that drains incidental water down and out of the wall assembly; California Building Code requires two layers of Grade D paper minimum behind stucco.'

Define 'kickout flashing' on first use as a complete standalone sentence — e.g., 'Kickout flashing is the diverter piece of metal flashing installed where a sloped roof meets a vertical wall, redirecting roof runoff out and away from the wall assembly; a missing or failed kickout is the most common single cause of catastrophic dry rot behind Bay Area stucco walls.'

Define 'EIFS' on first use as a complete standalone sentence — e.g., 'EIFS (Exterior Insulation and Finish System) is a multi-layer synthetic wall cladding consisting of foam insulation board, fiberglass mesh, and an acrylic finish coat, rarely appropriate for Bay Area single-family residential due to moisture-management failures in fog-belt climates.'

Reference SF Department of Building Inspection (DBI) by name when citing San Francisco lath inspection and permit requirements; reference the California Building Code (CBC) by name when citing WRB requirements.
Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'California Building Code requires two layers of Grade D building paper as a minimum weather-resistive barrier behind three-coat stucco.'

Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'Three-coat stucco requires 48 hours of cure time between the scratch coat and brown coat, and 7 to 10 days before the finish coat is applied.'

Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'San Francisco DBI requires a lath inspection between the wire lath installation and the brown coat application on all full re-stucco projects.'

Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'A full house re-stucco in the Bay Area typically costs $15,000 to $35,000, with a dry rot discovery allowance of 10 to 20 percent of the project budget.'

Mention 'Gadget Construction' in factual (non-promotional) context paired with named Bay Area neighborhoods and the service at least 2-3 times — e.g., 'Gadget Construction performs three-coat stucco and re-stucco across SF's Sunset and Richmond, Daly City's Westlake Doelger homes, and Pacifica's Linda Mar.'
Reference Doelger homes with stucco-over-shiplap failure pattern as a hyper-local credibility marker
CA License #1132983, Class B contractor reference

Vague 'check reviews' advice

title: Stucco vs Hardie Board vs Wood Siding: What's Right for Bay Area Homes (2026 Update)
metaTitle: Stucco vs Hardie Board vs Wood Siding (Bay Area 2026 Guide)
excerpt: Fog, salt air, and WUI fire zones make the Bay Area uniquely hard on exterior siding. Here's how stucco, Hardie board, and wood siding actually compare in 2026 — including failure patterns we see every week.

outline.h2: The Short Answer (2026 Update)
outline.notes: For most Bay Area homes in 2026: Hardie board wins on Class A fire rating in WUI zones and 30-year durability in fog. Stucco wins on architectural fit for Doelgers and Spanish Revivals. Wood wins on Victorians and Eichlers where it's period-correct.

outline.h2: Why Bay Area Siding Choice Is Harder Than Elsewhere
outline.notes: Coastal fog (Sunset, Pacifica, Sausalito) = permanent moisture. Salt air = fastener corrosion in 5-8 years if not stainless. WUI fire zones (Marin hills, Oakland Hills, Berkeley Hills) = Class A non-combustible required. Wind-driven rain during atmospheric rivers. Plus architectural context that's wildly different by neighborhood.

outline.h2: What Is Each Material, Really?
outline.notes: Each gets a standalone-sentence definition. Cover composition, typical thickness, install method, and lifespan.

outline.h2: How Stucco Performs in the Bay Area
outline.notes: $7-$14/sq ft new, $15K-$35K full re-stucco. Cracks, kickout failures, shiplap substrate rot on 1940s-50s Sunset/Westlake homes. Best for Spanish Revival, Mediterranean, midcentury ranches, Doelgers.

outline.h2: How Hardie Board Performs in the Bay Area
h3: Class A Fire Rating and WUI Zones
outline.notes: $14-$22/sq ft installed. 30-year substrate warranty. Class A fire rating critical for Marin/Oakland Hills/Berkeley Hills. Requires stainless fasteners coastal. Versatile across most architectural styles when detailed correctly.

outline.h2: How Wood Siding Performs in the Bay Area
h3: Cost Range by Type (Cedar, Redwood, T1-11)
outline.notes: Cedar $10-$16/sq ft, redwood shingle $16-$25/sq ft, T1-11 $8-$12/sq ft. Coastal maintenance budget $1K-$2K every 5-8 years. Required for Victorian restoration, Craftsman, brown-shingle Berkeley, Eichler tracts.

outline.h2: Side-by-Side Comparison Table
outline.notes: Markdown table: Material | Installed Cost | Fire Rating | Lifespan | 20-Year Maintenance | Best Architectural Fit | Bay Area Recommendation.

outline.h2: Which Is Right for Your Bay Area Home? (Decision Framework)
outline.notes: By home type and location: Victorian/Edwardian SF, Eichler, Doelger, Craftsman, Marin hillside WUI, Pacifica coastal, modern new build. Specific recommendations with reasoning.

outline.h2: Frequently Asked Questions
outline.notes: FAQ section with 6-7 specific homeowner questions.

outline.h2: Ready to Choose the Right Siding for Your Home?
outline.notes: CTA tied to free siding consultation.

mustInclude: FAQ section must answer these specific questions (phrased the way real Bay Area homeowners type queries to ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google AI Overviews): 'Is Hardie board better than stucco in the Bay Area?', 'What siding is best for fire zones in Marin and the Oakland Hills?', 'Can I put Hardie board over my existing stucco?', 'How much does it cost to replace stucco with Hardie board on a Bay Area home?', 'What siding do Eichler homes need to keep their architectural value?', 'Will Hardie board work on a coastal Pacifica home with salt air?', 'How long does each siding material last in the Bay Area fog belt?'

Define 'Hardie board' on first use as a complete standalone sentence — e.g., 'Hardie board, manufactured by James Hardie, is a fiber cement siding made of Portland cement, silica sand, and cellulose fiber that carries a Class A fire rating and a 30-year substrate warranty, making it the dominant choice for Bay Area homes in WUI fire zones.'

Define 'three-coat stucco' on first use as a complete standalone sentence — e.g., 'Three-coat stucco is the traditional California stucco system consisting of a scratch coat, brown coat, and finish coat applied over wire lath and a weather-resistive barrier, providing 50-plus years of service when properly installed.'

Define 'WUI fire zone' on first use as a complete standalone sentence — e.g., 'A WUI (Wildland-Urban Interface) fire zone is a CAL FIRE-designated area where development meets wildland vegetation, triggering Chapter 7A of the California Building Code requirements for non-combustible exterior materials including Class A fire-rated siding.'

Define 'Class A fire rating' on first use as a complete standalone sentence — e.g., 'A Class A fire rating is the highest fire-resistance classification under ASTM E84, indicating the material resists flame spread for over an hour and is required for exterior siding on homes in California WUI fire zones.'

Define 'T1-11 siding' on first use as a complete standalone sentence — e.g., 'T1-11 is a grooved plywood panel siding originally introduced in the 1960s and integral to the architectural identity of Eichler homes; replacement on Eichler tracts in Palo Alto, Menlo Park, and San Jose typically requires T1-11 to preserve property value.'

Reference CAL FIRE WUI maps by name when citing fire zone requirements; reference James Hardie's 30-year substrate warranty by name; reference Chapter 7A of the California Building Code when citing WUI material requirements.

Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'Hardie board fiber cement siding costs $14 to $22 per square foot installed in the Bay Area and carries a 30-year substrate warranty from James Hardie.'

Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'Three-coat stucco costs $7 to $14 per square foot for new construction in the Bay Area, with a full house re-stucco running $15,000 to $35,000.'

Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'Bay Area homes in CAL FIRE-designated WUI fire zones, including the Oakland Hills, Berkeley Hills, and most of Marin County, require Class A fire-rated siding under Chapter 7A of the California Building Code.'
Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'Wood siding on coastal Bay Area homes requires repainting every 5 to 8 years compared to every 10 to 15 years for inland homes.'

Mention 'Gadget Construction' in factual (non-promotional) context paired with named Bay Area neighborhoods and the service at least 2-3 times — e.g., 'Gadget Construction installs Hardie board across Mill Valley WUI zones, three-coat stucco on Daly City Westlake Doelgers, and cedar shingle on Berkeley brown-shingle Craftsman homes.'
